main_sections.title: A Bottle of Dew
main_sections.prose_summary: A Bottle of Dew is a folk tale by Sudha Murty set in a rural Indian context. Rama Natha, the son of a rich landlord, inherits large tracts of land but neglects them entirely because he is obsessed with finding a magic potion that can turn any object into gold. He spends his time and money chasing this fantasy, while his wife Madhumati grows increasingly worried about their finances. When a famous sage named Mahipati visits their town, Rama Natha immediately seeks his counsel about the potion. The sage, to Rama Natha's astonishment, claims to know how to make it: Rama Natha must plant banana plants, water them himself, and each winter collect the morning dew from the leaves by hand. When he has gathered five litres of dew, he must bring it to the sage to be enchanted. Concerned that this would take years, Rama Natha asks if he can plant more plants — the sage agrees but insists Rama Natha must do all the work himself. Rama Natha returns home and, talking it over with Madhumati, begins cleaning and cultivating his neglected fields. He plants rows of banana plants, tends them carefully, and every winter painstakingly collects the dew. Madhumati partners with him — she gathers the banana crop, sells it at the market, and earns good money. Over six years, they build a huge banana plantation, and Rama Natha finally collects his five litres of dew. He brings the bottle to the sage, who mutters over it and returns it, asking him to try it. When Rama Natha sprinkles the dew on a copper vessel and nothing turns to gold, he feels cheated. At that moment, Madhumati steps forward carrying a large box of gold coins — the wealth accumulated over six years of honest work. The sage reveals that there was never a magic potion; the story was a ruse to make Rama Natha work the land himself. His hard work, combined with Madhumati's enterprise in selling the fruit, created the wealth — not magic. The tale closes with Rama Natha deeply understanding this wisdom and resolving to work even harder.

reading_for_comprehension.section_name: Let us read + Let us discuss + Let us think and reflect

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Let us discuss (after Part I): Answer the following questions.

question_bank.stem: What did Rama Natha believe?
How did the sage help Rama Natha?
Do you think Rama Natha will be able to collect the dew? Give a reason.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Let us discuss (after Part II): Answer the following questions.

question_bank.stem: Why was Rama Natha angry?
How did Rama Natha and Madhumati create wealth?

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Let us think and reflect I: Read the following lines and answer the questions that follow.

question_bank.stem: Complete the sentence with a suitable reason. Rama Natha did not 'give up' because _____________________.

question_bank.table:
What Happened|Outcome
A. People promised to tell Rama Natha about the magic potion.|
B. Rama Natha was spending a lot of money.|

question_bank.stem: Write whether the following statements are True or False. A. Madhumati was troubled about what her husband did. B. Rama Natha was very happy digging his fields. C. The sage wanted to show the right path to Rama Natha.

Choose the option that lists the events given below in the correct order. A. The sage smiled. B. Rama Natha gave the bottle to the sage. C. Rama Natha dropped a few drops of water on a copper vessel. D. He said something in a low voice over the water. E. Rama Natha waited to see if the magic worked. (a) A, E, D, B, C  (b) B, A, D, C, E  (c) C, D, A, B, E  (d) A, D, B, E, C
Fill in the blank with the correct word from the lines given above. Seeing that the plant had dried up, the gardener ____________ some water on it.
How might Rama Natha have felt when nothing happened to the copper vessel?

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Let us think and reflect II: Answer the following questions.

question_bank.stem: What did the sage ask Rama Natha to do to make the magic potion?
Why did the sage ask Rama Natha to do everything himself?
How could Rama Natha have a big banana plantation after six years?
How did the sage make Rama Natha believe that there is no magic potion?
Fill in the 'before' and 'after' table about Rama Natha.
question_bank.table:
What he did before meeting the sage|What he did after meeting the sage
(i)|(i)
(ii)|(ii)

listening.transcript_text: Madhumati is very happy as she has many big bags of bananas. Firstly, she visits her mother and gives her two dozen bananas. Secondly, she goes to the market to sell the bananas. Next, she meets her old friend Kalawati in the market. Then, she gifts Kalawati a dozen bananas. Later, she decides to go home as she has sold most of the bananas. After that, on the way home, she meets a little boy who is hungry and gives him two bananas. Lastly, she goes to her neighbour's house and gives them a bunch of bananas. She has a smile on her face as she goes home.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: You will listen to what Madhumati does with the bananas from her plantation. As you listen, number the events in the correct order in which they happen. Write the number in the box beside each event.

question_bank.stem: Gives two bananas to a boy who is hungry
Meets her friend Kalawati in the market
Visits her mother and gives her two dozen bananas
Chooses to go home as only some bananas were left
Visits her neighbour and gifts them a bunch of bananas
Gives twelve bananas to her friend
Goes to the market to sell the bananas

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Read aloud the following words from the story: sage, promising, wisdom, trees, surprise, beds, sand, clothes, sea, was, keys, bags, nose. All these words have the letter 's' in them. But the sound of letter 's' is not always the same. In sage and promising the letter 's' has a /s/ sound; in wisdom and trees the letter 's' has a /z/ sound; in surprise the first letter 's' has a /s/ sound and the second has a /z/ sound. Now, speak each word clearly along with your teacher and classmates. Your teacher will now say some words. Listen carefully to the sound of 's'. Write the sound next to the word.

Discuss the following questions with a partner. You may use the hints given in brackets while speaking. 1. What does hard work mean to you? (It means … / It is something … / Well, you see … /What I mean is …) 2. Give three reasons why you think hard work is important. (Firstly, I feel … because … / Secondly, I think … since … /Lastly, I believe … as … ) 3. Share three ideas you would give to someone who needs to work hard. (To begin with, it is important that … /Next, you could … / Finally, I think you should … )

question_bank.stem: What does hard work mean to you?
Give three reasons why you think hard work is important.
Share three ideas you would give to someone who needs to work hard.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Work in pairs to complete the description of a banana. You may take the help of the words given in brackets (covering: when I like to eat a banana, smell, healthy because, how the outer covering feels on touch, taste, colour of the outer covering and fruit). Now, use the information given in the picture to write eight sentences about a banana. Share your responses with your classmates and teacher.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: On the basis of the story 'A Bottle of Dew' develop a script with dialogues and enact it with expressions. Present it in the assembly or your classroom.

question_bank.stem: Develop a script with dialogues based on 'A Bottle of Dew' and enact it.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Read the following words from the story: promised, spending, cheated, luck, remember, huge, difficult, market. Use the words correctly to complete the paragraph given below. There are two extra words in the box that you do not need.

question_bank.stem: There was a farmer who had a ________ coconut plantation. He worked hard and did not depend on ________. Every day was ________ but he did not give up. One day, a man bought a bag of coconuts from him, and ________ to pay him the money the next day. Days passed and the man did not pay him anything. The farmer felt ________. About two months later, he met the same man and asked him about his money. The man said, 'Oh! I had forgotten. Now, I ________ buying coconuts from you.' And he paid the money.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Read the words from the story in Column A. Read the new words in Column B. Complete the meanings of the words given in Column B. The words given in each row have the same sounds but different spellings and meanings. Such words are called homophones.

question_bank.stem: Complete the meanings of the Column B homophones: eye (meaning of I), due (meaning of dew), sun (meaning of son), eight (meaning of ate), sea (meaning of see).
question_bank.table:
Column A|Column B|Meaning of words in Column B
I|eye|
your|you're|short form of 'you are'
dew|due|happening at some time in the future
son|sun|
one|won|past tense of win
ate|eight|
see|sea|

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Choose four pairs of homophones from the table above, and for each pair write a sentence that uses both homophones in the same sentence. Check your answers by sharing them with your teacher and classmates. An example has been given: I ate eight bananas for breakfast today.

question_bank.stem: Write four sentences each using a pair of homophones from the table.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Match the phrases in Column A with the phrases in Column B. Use the connecting words given in the middle column (because, before, as soon as, as) to make meaningful sentences. Now, write the new sentences in your notebook.

question_bank.stem: Match: The children submitted their work (When?) — the bell rang. / He did not want to try rock climbing (Why?) — there was a traffic jam. / The children ran towards the gate (When?) — the deadline. / Nitin was late to school today (Why?) — of his fear of heights.

He did not want to try rock climbing (Why?)||there was a traffic jam.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Here are two sentences with letter 'b'. Read them slowly the first time. As you read them a few more times, read them faster each time. 'Bunty bhaiya bought a big bunch of bananas.' 'A big bunch of bananas was bought by Bunty bhaiya.' Now, try to create a tongue twister on your own and share it with your peers.

main_sections.title: The Raven and the Fox
main_sections.poem_text:
Mr Raven was perched upon a limb,
And Reynard the Fox looked up at him;
For the Raven held in his great big beak,
A morsel the Fox would go far to seek.

Said the Fox, in admiring tones: "My word!
Sir Raven, you are a handsome bird.
Such feathers! If you would only sing,
The birds of these woods would call you King."

The Raven, who did not see the joke,
Forgot that his voice was just a croak.
He opened his beak, in his foolish pride–
And down fell the morsel the Fox had eyed.

"Ha-ha!" laughed the Fox. "And now you know,
Ignore sweet words that make you glow.
Pride, my friend, is rather unwise;
I'm sure this teaching is quite a surprise."

Jean de La Fontaine
main_sections.poem_appreciation_summary: This four-stanza poem is a classic beast fable adapted from Jean de La Fontaine's version of the Aesop tale. The central theme is the danger of vanity and pride. A raven perched on a branch clutches a tasty morsel of food. A cunning fox below flatters the raven with praise about his looks and feathers, then suggests that if only the raven would sing, he would be crowned king of the forest. The raven, blinded by his pride, opens his beak to sing and drops his food. The fox takes it and departs with a moralising laugh: pride is unwise, and one should not be swayed by flattery. The dominant literary devices are rhyming couplets (limb/him, beak/seek, word/bird, etc.) and alliteration (great big beak, song of sweet birds). The tone shifts from admiring flattery in stanzas one and two to ironic triumph in stanza four. The moral — that excessive pride makes one vulnerable to manipulation — is made explicit by the fox himself in the final stanza.

reading_for_comprehension.section_name: Let us read + Let us discuss + Let us think and reflect

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Let us discuss I: Fill in the blanks by choosing the correct option, stanza by stanza.

question_bank.stem: Stanza 1: (i) Mr Raven is a __________. (bird/frog) (ii) The name of the fox is __________. (Raven/Reynard) (iii) Mr Raven had __________ in his beak. (a piece of food/a bunch of leaves)

Stanza 2: (i) The Fox calls the Raven a __________ bird. (foolish/good-looking) (ii) The Fox asks the Raven to __________. (dance/sing) (iii) The Raven and the Fox live in the __________. (trees/woods)
Stanza 3: (i) The Raven ___________ that he croaked. (remembered/forgot) (ii) The Raven opened its beak to ___________. (eat/sing) (iii) The ___________ fell down. (food/Raven)
Stanza 4: (i) The Fox ___________ at the Raven. (laughed/looked) (ii) It is not ___________ to be too proud of oneself. (wise/unwise) (iii) The Raven learnt a ______________. (lesson/teaching)

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Let us discuss II: Read the poem again and match the words in Column A with their meanings in Column B.

question_bank.stem: Match: 1. perched, 2. morsel, 3. seek, 4. pride, 5. eyed, 6. limb, 7. woods — with their meanings.
question_bank.table:
Column A|Column B
perched|(i) a small piece of food
morsel|(ii) looked with interest at something
seek|(iii) feeling that you are better than others
pride|(iv) sat on a branch
eyed|(v) to look for something
limb|(vi) a smaller area of forest with similar kind of trees
woods|(vii) branch of a tree

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Let us think and reflect I: Look at the pictures and number them in the order that they happen in the poem.

question_bank.stem: Number the four pictures in the order they appear in the poem.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Let us think and reflect II: Read the following lines and answer the questions that follow.

question_bank.stem: From lines 'Sir Raven, you are a handsome bird. Such feathers! If you would only sing, The birds of these woods would call you King.' — (i) 'Such feathers' refer to __________ feathers. A. shiny and beautiful B. black and dull C. grey and long D. short and unattractive (ii) Why would the birds of the woods call the Raven 'King'? (iii) Why does the Fox address the Raven as 'Sir'?

From lines 'The Raven, who did not see the joke, Forgot that his voice was just a croak. He opened his beak, in his foolish pride–' — (i) Why did the Raven open his beak? (ii) Complete the following with one word from the given lines. Fox : clever : : Raven : ______________
Why does the Raven forget that his voice is just a croak?
How does Reynard make the Raven sing?
Why does Reynard say that pride is not wise?
Give one reason why the teaching is quite a surprise.
Imagine someone praises you too much. How would you react?

listening.transcript_text: Dear students, the crow felt really sad because the fox cheated him. In the quiet night, the crow thought and said to himself, 'I was fooled by kind words, but I've learned something important. Even if I don't have beautiful feathers, I'm smart.' To share his wisdom, he decided to go on a journey. He wanted to tell all the other animals to be careful with words. He told them, 'Do not let pride lose what you have.'

tasks_verbatim.task_text: You will listen to what the crow did after he lost his food. As you listen, mark the given statements as True or False.

question_bank.stem: The crow was unhappy that he lost his food.
The crow thought that his lovely feathers made him smart.
The crow wanted to tell his friends not to be proud.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Narrate the poem in the form of a story. Give a different ending to the story. You may begin like this: This is a story about a clever fox named Reynard and Mr Raven. One day…

question_bank.stem: Narrate the poem as a story with a different ending.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Now, write the story you narrated in the form of a conversation. (Remember to write only the actions for the Raven as he has food in his mouth and does not speak.) You may begin like this: Reynard: Good morning, Mr Raven! You seem to be awake so early! (Mr Raven just nods his head) Reynard: Oh, Mr Raven, you look …

question_bank.stem: Write the narrated story as a conversation/dialogue script.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Write the rhyming words from the poem. One has been done for you. Stanza 1: (i) limb-him (ii)______ ______; Stanza 2: (i) word-______ (ii)______ ______; Stanza 3: (i) joke-______ (ii)______ ______; Stanza 4: (i) know-______ (ii)______ ______

question_bank.stem: Identify the rhyming word pairs from each stanza of the poem.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Study the underlined words in the poem. 'For the Raven held in his great big beak.' The words 'big' and 'beak' begin with the same /b/ sound. 'The song of sweet birds.' Alliteration is a repetition of consonant sounds at the beginning of words. When two or more words together begin with the same sound, it is called alliteration. Now, pick another example of alliteration from Stanza 2.

question_bank.stem: Pick another example of alliteration from Stanza 2 of the poem.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Choose the correct opposites from the box given below and complete the table. There are two extra words that you will not need. (wise, happy, humility, flew, remembered, notice, dim, cried, silly) Then use any four words from the table to make sentences of your own.

question_bank.stem: Match each word with its opposite: perched, forgot, foolish, pride, laughed, ignore, glow.
question_bank.table:
Words|Opposites
perched|
forgot|
foolish|
pride|
laughed|
ignore|
glow|

question_bank.stem: Use any four words from the opposites table to make sentences of your own.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Origami is the art of folding objects out of paper to create both two-dimensional and three-dimensional objects. Let us make stick puppets and enact the poem. You may take the help of the pictures given below. Note: You may use the puppets while narrating the story.

question_bank.stem: Make origami stick puppets of the Raven and the Fox using the step-by-step pictures provided, then enact the poem.

main_sections.title: Rama to the Rescue
main_sections.prose_summary: Rama to the Rescue is a comic-strip folk tale from Tamil Nadu, adapted from Amar Chitra Katha, scripted by Luis M. Fernandes with illustrations by Ram Waeerkar. The story is set in a village at night. A husband and wife are getting ready to sleep when the wife notices that someone is trying to break into their house. The husband realises a thief has already got in and whispers a plan to his wife: they will pretend to have a conversation about naming their unborn child. If the boy is inside the house, the wife will call the name softly; if he is in the yard, a little louder; and if he is in the street, very loudly. The name they choose is 'Rama' — which is also the name of the village kotwal (police officer). The thief overhears what he thinks is a domestic chat and is frustrated that they are not talking about their money. As the conversation progresses and the wife calls 'Rama!' loudly, Rama, the actual village kotwal who is on patrol, hears his name being called and runs to the house. He discovers that a thief has dug his way in, surprises the intruder, and arrests him. The man and his wife celebrate — their clever plan of using the kotwal's name as a distress signal, without directly alerting the thief, saved them from being robbed. The story celebrates quick wit and cleverness in the face of danger.

reading_for_comprehension.section_name: Let us read + Let us discuss + Let us think and reflect

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Let us discuss (after first comic sequence): Circle the odd one. 1. The characters are: husband, wife, children. 2. Who entered the house? guest, robber, Kotwal.

question_bank.stem: Circle the odd one: 1. The characters are: husband, wife, children. 2. Who entered the house? guest, robber, Kotwal.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Let us discuss (after second comic sequence): Write whether True or False. 1. The man tells his plan to his wife. 2. The thief wants to know where they kept their jewels.

question_bank.stem: Write True or False: 1. The man tells his plan to his wife. 2. The thief wants to know where they kept their jewels.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Let us discuss (mid-story): 1. Along with your classmates, call out the name 'Rama' – softly, loudly and very loudly. 2. What do you think will happen next in the story?

What do you think will happen next in the story?

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Let us discuss (end of story): Match the words in Column A with the meanings in Column B.

question_bank.stem: Match: 1. wonder, 2. yard, 3. chatter, 4. instead, 5. closely, 6. perhaps — with their meanings.
question_bank.table:
Column A|Column B
wonder|(i) in place of someone or something
yard|(ii) carefully
chatter|(iii) an area outside a building
instead|(iv) talk for a long time about unimportant things
closely|(v) maybe
perhaps|(vi) a feeling of surprise

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Let us discuss: Fill in the boxes with suitable words as you read the story — Main Characters, Setting, Other Characters, Problem, Solution.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Let us think and reflect I: Read the following lines and answer the questions that follow.

question_bank.stem: From the lines 'Man: What's the matter? What are you looking at? / Wife: Ssssh! Listen! / Man: Somebody is trying to get in. / Wife: I … I think he has got in … the noise has stopped.' — (i) What makes the man say the first line? (ii) The wife says 'Ssssh! Listen!'. What does the word 'Ssssh' mean here? A. sleep B. help C. rest D. be silent (iii) How can you tell that the wife was listening carefully?

From the lines 'Rama, the village kotwal, ran to the house from which he heard his name being called. Rama: Oh, Oh! Some thief has dug his way into this house.' — (i) Fill in the blank with the correct word from the given lines: The villagers caught the thief and took him to the _____________________. (ii) What do the words 'Oh, Oh!' show here? A. surprise B. anger C. pain D. happiness (iii) How can you tell that the kotwal was good at his job?

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Let us think and reflect II: Answer the following questions.

question_bank.stem: Give one reason why the man and his wife were not able to fall asleep.
What did the thief wish would happen? Did it happen?
How did the man and his wife get help from the kotwal?
Do you think cleverness can help us solve our problems? Why do you say so?

listening.transcript_text: Attention, all! There are reports of robbery in our locality. Please be alert and listen to the instructions carefully. Lock all doors and windows at night and also when you leave the house. Children, you must inform any suspicious activity to adults immediately. Do not talk to strangers or get anything from them. Report anything unusual. Always remember that safety is important. Dial 100 in case of emergency.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: You will listen to a security guard making an announcement to the people in the neighbourhood. As you listen, fill in the blanks with only one word that you hear.

question_bank.stem: Fill in blanks: The security guard announced that 1. ____________ has been happening in that area. He advised everyone to stay safe and the kids to tell the 2. ____________ as soon as they find anything different. They should not forget that 3. ____________ is the main thing. He asked them to call 4. ____________ when they need immediate help.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Listen to your teacher pronounce the contractions and repeat after your teacher: I'll, it's, don't, I'm, I'd, he's, that's, we'll, they're, you're, we're, what's. We use contracted form of the verbs while speaking and expanded form while writing. Now, speak these contractions clearly along with your teacher and classmates: can't, won't, I've, wouldn't, shouldn't, didn't.

Discuss in pairs: 1. Why do you think it's important for people to keep their house and themselves safe? Tick the suitable reasons. 2. Now, use the given hints to share your reasons in complete sentences. 3. Use the given hints to share three ideas on how people can keep their houses and themselves safe.

question_bank.stem: Why is it important to keep your house and yourself safe? Discuss and share in complete sentences.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: The next day, the neighbour asked the man in the story about what had happened. As the man, tell the neighbour what had happened in your own words. Remember to include: when, where, what, how, why. You may begin this way: My wife and I were about to go to sleep last night when we heard someone …

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Expand the following contracted words. Two examples have been done for you (what's = what is; must've = must have). Use the contracted form of these words in sentences of your own: I'll, I'd, we'll, that's, we're, don't, you'll.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Study the highlighted words in sentences from the story: 1. I am feeling so sleepy. 2. What are you looking at? 3. Somebody is trying to get in. 4. They are saying something. 5. Perhaps, they are talking about their money. Now choose the correct option: The sentences 1–5 refer to actions that _________________ [are going on at the time of speaking / happened in the past / will take place in the future]. Verbs that describe what someone or something is doing at the moment of speaking are in the present progressive tense.

options: are going on at the time of speaking
will take place in the future

[is+read+ing] 3. We _______ a board game. [are+play+ing] 4. I _______ a new language. [am+learn+ing] 5. The teachers _______ in the staff room. [are+sit+ing] 6. My mother _______ food in the kitchen. [is+cook+ing]

Anil _________ the score card. 4. The mother _________ a book. 5. The dog _________ to catch the ball. 6. The birds _________ in the sky.

Study the sentences and complete the adverb table: (i) I'd better listen closely — How does he listen? (ii) When he's in the house, I'll call out softly to him — How does she call out? (iii) Then I'll call out very loudly — How does she call out?

S.No.|Sentence|Question|Word
(i)|I'd better listen closely.|How does he listen?|A. closely
(ii)|When he's in the house, I'll call out softly to him.|How does she call out?|B.

question_bank.stem: Fill in blanks with suitable adverbs (honestly, brightly, peacefully, sweetly, angrily, slowly, tirelessly): 1. He solved the mathematics problem _____. 2. The car moved _____ along the highway. 3. The musician played the guitar _____. 4. Shobha danced _____. 5. Rohit left the house _____.

Fill in blanks with suitable adverbs (honestly, brightly, peacefully, sweetly, angrily, slowly, tirelessly) in the forest passage: Once upon a time in a small forest, animals lived 1. _____. The wise old tortoise moved 2. _____ but always won the race. The ants worked 3. _____, gathering food for the winter. The kind-hearted lion ruled 4. _____. The birds sang 5. _____.

Study the sentences and choose the correct option: (i) 'Oh, hum! What a hard day it has been!' expresses a feeling of _____ (tiredness/joy). (ii) 'Ah, at last, they've stopped their chatter!' expresses a feeling of _____ (wonder/relief). (iii) 'What should we do?' and 'What should we name our child?' ask _____ (answers/questions).

Punctuate the following sentences and write whether they are exclamatory or interrogative: 1. Wow, what an intelligent man he is  2. Oh how big the hole is  3. Do you like to read comic books  4. What is your favourite story

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Find out from your teacher or parents what you would do in the following situations. Who will you call if … you see fire / you see someone getting hurt / you see a wounded animal / you see someone suspicious / you find a lost child / you get lost.

question_bank.stem: Find out and note down who to call in each emergency situation listed.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Find the right path for the kotwal to catch the thief (maze activity).

question_bank.stem: Solve the maze to find the right path for the kotwal to reach the thief.
